mcqs.question: What is the primary performance bottleneck in the basic CUDA trapezoidal rule that version II addresses?

options.text: Excessive use of shared memory causing bank conflicts
Serialization of atomicAdd operations on a single global memory location
Too many cudaMemcpy calls between host and device
Insufficient number of threads launched in the grid

mcqs.explanation: In version I, every thread calls atomicAdd on the same global memory address. Since only one atomic operation can complete at a time on that address, all N threads serialize, making the accumulation O(N) despite being launched in parallel.

mcqs.question: In the tree-structured shared memory reduction with blockDim.x = 256, how many reduction steps are needed to produce the block sum?
mcqs.explanation: The tree reduction halves active threads at each step, requiring log2(256) = 8 steps to reduce 256 values to a single sum.

mcqs.question: What happens if __syncthreads() is omitted between reduction steps in the shared memory reduction?

options.text: The kernel runs faster but produces the same result
Threads may read stale values from shared memory, causing non-deterministic incorrect results
Only thread 0 is affected while other threads compute correctly

mcqs.explanation: Without __syncthreads(), a thread may read a shared memory location before another thread has written its updated value from the previous reduction step. This race condition leads to non-deterministic and incorrect results.

mcqs.question: Why is stride-halving reduction preferred over stride-doubling reduction in CUDA?

options.text: Stride-halving avoids warp divergence and shared memory bank conflicts

mcqs.explanation: Stride-halving keeps adjacent threads (low threadIdx values) active and accessing adjacent memory locations. Stride-doubling uses a modulo condition that causes warp divergence (threads within the same warp take different paths) and accesses non-adjacent memory locations causing bank conflicts.

mcqs.question: In the improved kernel, how many global memory writes does each block perform to store its reduction result?
mcqs.explanation: After the shared memory reduction completes, only thread 0 of each block writes the final block sum (sdata[0]) to blockSums[blockIdx.x] in global memory. This replaces up to blockDim.x atomic global memory operations with a single store.
